stem: A company offers two annual software licensing plans. Under Plan X, the total annual cost $C$ (in dollars) for $n$ users satisfies $C(3) = 620$ and $C(8) = 820$. Under Plan Y, the total annual cost is $C(n) = 70n + 200$ dollars. For how many users $n$ does Plan Y cost exactly $\$90$ more per year than Plan X?

distractorRationale.A: Sets up the difference in the wrong direction, computing X(n) - Y(n) = 90 instead of Y(n) - X(n) = 90: solves (40n + 500) - (70n + 200) = 90, giving -30n + 300 = 90, -30n = -210, and n = 7.
distractorRationale.B: Uses the given data point C(3) = 620 as the y-intercept of Plan X instead of computing it: writes X(n) = 40n + 620; then Y(n) - X(n) = 30n - 420 = 90 giving 30n = 510 and n = 17.
distractorRationale.C: Solves for when both plans cost the same (difference = 0) instead of when the difference is $90: sets Y(n) = X(n), giving 70n + 200 = 40n + 500, 30n = 300, and n = 10.
distractorRationale.D: Correct: finds Plan X's slope as 40 and y-intercept as 500 giving X(n) = 40n + 500, then solves Y(n) - X(n) = 90, i.e., 30n - 300 = 90, to get n = 13.
